title: See the Climactic Ending of Steven Spielbergãs Breakout Duel Recreated Entirely with 3D-Printed Models
description:
in Film |   	March 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

With his last picôÙture The FabelôÙmans, Steven SpielôÙberg told a stoôÙry of his own.ô GivôÙen h
body:
in Film |   	March 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

With his last picôÙture The FabelôÙmans, Steven SpielôÙberg told a stoôÙry of his own.ô GivôÙen his long-held stature as more or less the perôÙsonôÙiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion of big-screen HolôÙlyôÙwood enterôÙtainôÙment, thereãs only one such stoôÙry he could have told: that of how he became a filmôÙmakôÙer. The most memôÙoôÙrable of The FabelôÙmans depicts the young direcôÙtoôÙrôÙiôÙal surôÙroôÙgate alone in the baseôÙment of his famôÙiôÙly home, re-creôÙatôÙing the train crash scene from The GreatôÙest Show on Earth with an eight-milôÙlimeôÙter camôÙera and a Lionel set.ô Today, on the brink of his ninth decade with his famous proôÙducôÙtivôÙiôÙty hardôÙly slowôÙing, SpielôÙberg remains, on some levôÙel, the wide-eyed boy smashôÙing his toys togethôÙer at just the right angle.ô What betôÙter way to pay him tribôÙute than to repliôÙcate his cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic achieveôÙments in miniaôÙture?
The FabelôÙmansô ends with its proôÙtagôÙoôÙnist a colôÙlege stuôÙdent, eager to drop out and go straight to HolôÙlyôÙwood. At the same point in life, the real SpielôÙberg was about to receive an offer from UniôÙverôÙsal PicôÙtures to write and direct the short film that became Amblinã, which itself led to a conôÙtract to direct teleôÙviôÙsion proôÙducôÙtions.
He showed what he could do with episodes of MarôÙcus WelôÙby, M.D., The Name of the Game, and ColumôÙbo, among othôÙer series. Then he stepped up to TV movies, aô form regardôÙed asô infeôÙriôÙor in all respects to theôÙatriôÙcal releasôÙes, but one he manôÙaged to tranôÙscend on the first try. When it first aired in 1971 as an ABC Movie of the Week, Duelô preôÙsentôÙed its viewôÙers with a harôÙrowôÙing, near-mythoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal conôÙfrontaôÙtion between a midôÙdle-aged travôÙelôÙing salesôÙman in a PlyôÙmouth Valiant and an unseen truckôÙer in a hulkôÙing, smoke-belchôÙing big rig who seems bent on destroyôÙing him.


	

GivôÙen that its direcôÙtor was just 24 years old at the time, Duel very much counts as earôÙly SpielôÙberg. Yet itãs also disôÙtilled SpielôÙberg, a head-on treatôÙment of midôÙdle-class norôÙmalôÙiôÙtyãs sudôÙden encounter with a force of incomôÙpreôÙhenôÙsiôÙble menôÙace ã a theme much revisôÙitôÙed in his work since ã with cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic rhythms preôÙciseôÙly calôÙcuôÙlatôÙed for optiôÙmal tenôÙsion and release. An aspirôÙing filmôÙmakôÙer could learn much from re-creôÙatôÙing its sequences shot-for-shot. The YouTube chanôÙnel Movies MiniaôÙtures Effects does just that in the video above, which docôÙuôÙments a remakôÙing with 3D-printôÙed maqueôÙttes of the final crash, after DenôÙnis Weaverãs desôÙperôÙate everyôÙman manôÙages to outôÙwit his purôÙsuer. ãSheer skill needôÙed more phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy for a fitôÙting resôÙoôÙluôÙtion,ã wrote David ThomôÙson of this endôÙing. PerôÙhaps so, but the more than 18 milôÙlion views so far racked up by its miniaôÙture verôÙsion do sugôÙgest a film that more than retains its powôÙer after 45 years.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Watch Steven Spielbergãs Rarely Seen 1968 Film Amblinã
Watch Steven Spielbergãs Debut: Two Films He DirectôÙed as a TeenagôÙer
Shot-By-Shot BreakôÙdowns of Spielbergãs FilmôÙmakôÙing in Jaws, Scorseseãs in Cape Fear, and De Palmaãs in The UntouchôÙables
How Movies CreôÙatôÙed Their SpeôÙcial Effects Before CGI: MetropôÙoôÙlis, 2001: A Space Odyssey & More
How Wes AnderôÙson Uses MiniaôÙtures to CreôÙate His AesôÙthetôÙic: A Primer from His ModôÙel MakôÙer & Prop Painter
How Car Chase Scenes Have Evolved Over 100 Years
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: How the Hoover Dam Works: A 3D Animated Introduction
description:
in History, Technology |   	March 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When it comes to tourist pilôÙgrimôÙage sites in the UnitôÙed States, the Hoove
body:
in History, Technology |   	March 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When it comes to tourist pilôÙgrimôÙage sites in the UnitôÙed States, the Hoover Dam may not quite rank up there with the StatôÙue of LibôÙerôÙty, the LinôÙcoln MemoôÙrôÙiôÙal, Mount RushôÙmore, the Grand Canyon, or DisôÙneyôÙland. But thatãs not due to a lack of imporôÙtance, nor even a lack of impresôÙsiveôÙness. PropôÙer appreôÙciôÙaôÙtion of its man-made majesty, howôÙevôÙer, requires an underôÙstandôÙing of not just the vital funcôÙtion it serves, but the enorôÙmous task of its conôÙstrucôÙtion. The guides at the Hoover Dam have been trained to explain just that to its many visôÙiôÙtors, of course, but all of us could benôÙeôÙfit from going in preôÙpared with a litôÙtle knowlôÙedge. Watch the hour-long video on the damãs design and conôÙstrucôÙtion from AniôÙmaôÙgraffs above, and you may be preôÙpared with enough knowlôÙedge to tell the guides a thing or two.
AniôÙmaôÙgraffs is the YoTôÙtube chanôÙnel of Jacob OãNeal, which weãve preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured here on Open CulôÙture for its acclaimed explaôÙnaôÙtions onô a sixôÙteenth-cenôÙtuôÙry explorôÙerãs sailôÙing ship and the GoldôÙen Gate Bridge, anothôÙer iconôÙicô conôÙstrucôÙtion project ofô the Great DepresôÙsion. Like those, his Hoover Dam video uses detailed 3D modôÙels based on seriôÙous research, not least into the proôÙjecôÙtãs origôÙiôÙnal design docôÙuôÙments.
This allows OãNeal to show each eleôÙment of the dam and its comôÙplex sysôÙtem of supôÙportôÙing infraôÙstrucôÙture in detail and from every angle, as well as in a kind of xãray vision. Weãve all seen phoôÙtographs of the Hoover Dam, and maybe even bought some from its gift shop, but even the most subôÙlime aerôÙiôÙal view doesôÙnãt reveal as much about its ambiôÙtion as a look into its inner workôÙings.
And the ambiôÙtion of the Hoover Dam is one aspect guarôÙanôÙteed to impress any viewôÙers. It required thouôÙsands of workôÙers about five years to re-shape the NevaôÙda and AriôÙzona landôÙscape at a grand enough scale to make posôÙsiôÙble human conôÙtrol of the mighty ã and, more to the point, mightôÙiôÙly unpreôÙdictable ã ColôÙorado RivôÙer. With its large turôÙbines, the engiôÙneerôÙing and instalôÙlaôÙtion of which OãNeal explains in full, it manôÙaged to genôÙerôÙate enough elecôÙtricôÙiôÙty to repay its conôÙstrucôÙtion cost of more than $811 milôÙlion in todayãs dolôÙlars by 1987, just over 50 years after it opened. And in an achieveôÙment almost imposôÙsiôÙble to believe today, it opened more than two years ahead of schedôÙule. We hear a good deal today about the conôÙcept of ãstate capacôÙiôÙty,ã and how the U.S. could regain it. At the Hoover Dam, we behold state capacôÙiôÙty quite litôÙerôÙalôÙly made conôÙcrete.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
The IncredôÙiôÙble StoôÙry of the Hoover Dam
The Genius Urban Design of AmsôÙterôÙdam: Canals, Dams & LeanôÙing HousôÙes
How Medieval IslamôÙic EngiôÙneerôÙing Brought Water to the AlhamôÙbra
The Genius EngiôÙneerôÙing of Roman AqueôÙducts
The BrilôÙliant EngiôÙneerôÙing That Made Venice: How a City Was Built on Water
DisôÙcovôÙer Ansel Adamsã 226 PhoôÙtos of U.S. NationôÙal Parks (and AnothôÙer Side of the LegôÙendary PhoôÙtogôÙraôÙphôÙer)
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: An Introduction to Brutalism: The Iconic Postwar Architectural Style That Combined Utopianism and Concrete
description:
in Architecture, History |   	March 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The artiôÙfiôÙcial lanôÙguage of EsperanôÙto was conôÙceived with high ideals i
body:
in Architecture, History |   	March 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The artiôÙfiôÙcial lanôÙguage of EsperanôÙto was conôÙceived with high ideals in mind. In the eighôÙteen-eightôÙies, its creôÙator L. L. ZamenôÙhof enviôÙsioned it as the uniôÙverôÙsal secôÙond lanôÙguage of humanôÙiôÙty, and if it hasôÙnãt achieved that staôÙtus by now, it at least remains the worldãs most wideôÙly spoôÙken conôÙstructôÙed auxôÙilôÙiary lanôÙguage. One facôÙtor comôÙpliôÙcatôÙing its spread is that no lanôÙguage, even one guidôÙed by interôÙnaôÙtionôÙalôÙism, can remain the same for long enough in two difôÙferôÙent culôÙtures. As in spoôÙken and writôÙten lanôÙguages, so in the conôÙcrete one of archiôÙtecôÙture ã and in the case of the style known as BruôÙtalôÙism, that would be litôÙerôÙal conôÙcrete. Meant to make humanôÙiôÙty whole again after the SecôÙond World War, its buildôÙings endôÙed up being rather more parôÙticôÙuôÙlar, and less utopiôÙan, than their archiôÙtects intendôÙed.
ExamôÙples aplenôÙty appear in the new video above from Built NarôÙraôÙtive, which offers what amounts to a postôÙcard tour of BruôÙtalôÙist (and BruôÙtalôÙism-adjaôÙcent) buildôÙings from around the world. Named for its main mateôÙrôÙiôÙal bûˋton brut, or raw conôÙcrete, the style came into its own durôÙing the rebuildôÙing of war-ruined secôÙtions of British and conôÙtiôÙnenôÙtal EuroôÙpean cities ã and, over in the U.S., the rapid proôÙlifôÙerôÙaôÙtion and expanôÙsion of colôÙlege camôÙpusôÙes ã which had to be done quickôÙly and under less-than-extravôÙaôÙgant budôÙgets.
Libraries, research facilôÙiôÙties, city halls, adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtive buildôÙings, courôÙtôÙhousôÙes, housôÙing projects: these are the sorts of strucôÙtures that most often took BruôÙtalôÙist form in the nineôÙteen-fifties, sixôÙties, and sevôÙenôÙties, resultôÙing in the instiôÙtuôÙtionôÙal, bureauôÙcratôÙic, and in some places totalôÙiôÙtarôÙiôÙan assoôÙciôÙaôÙtions it still has today.
Some pubôÙlicly loathed BruôÙtalôÙist buildôÙings, like the TriôÙcorn CenôÙtre in Portsmouth and theô Third Church of Christ, SciôÙenôÙtist in WashôÙingôÙton, D.C. have been torn down, often after decades of negôÙliôÙgent mainôÙteôÙnance. OthôÙers, like the BarôÙbiôÙcan Estate in LonôÙdon or HabiôÙtat 67 in MonôÙtreôÙal, are now beloved sites of pilôÙgrimôÙage. WideôÙly acknowlôÙedged masôÙters of BruôÙtalôÙism include Le CorôÙbusier,ô who pioôÙneered it with buildôÙings like the Unitûˋ dãHabiôÙtaôÙtion in MarôÙseille (not Berlin, conôÙtra the capôÙtion in the video) and KenôÙzo Tange (proôÙnounced ãtawn-gay,ã not ãtangã as the narôÙraôÙtor says it), whose work steered the JapanôÙese verôÙsion of the moveôÙment in its own subôÙtle, someôÙtimes playôÙful direcôÙtions. Now, thanks in part to the rapid difôÙfuôÙsion of archiôÙtecôÙturôÙal phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphy made posôÙsiôÙble by social media, a new enthuôÙsiôÙast of BruôÙtalôÙism seems to be born every minute. Even if they donãt believe thatô archiôÙtecôÙture can bring a new world into being, they still feel the pull of aô future that nevôÙer came ã or, at any rate, hasôÙnãt come yet.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
EveryôÙthing You Ever WantôÙed to Know About the BeauôÙty of BruôÙtalôÙist ArchiôÙtecôÙture: An IntroôÙducôÙtion in Six Videos
Why PeoôÙple Hate BruôÙtalôÙist BuildôÙings on AmerôÙiôÙcan ColôÙlege CamôÙpusôÙes
Why Do PeoôÙple Hate ModôÙern ArchiôÙtecôÙture?: A Video Essay
GoodôÙbye to the NakôÙaôÙgin CapôÙsule TowôÙer, Tokyoãs Strangest and Most UtopiôÙan ApartôÙment BuildôÙing
The World AccordôÙing to Le CorôÙbusier: An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the Most ModôÙern of All ArchiôÙtects
An EspresôÙso MakôÙer Made in Le Corbusierãs BruôÙtalôÙist ArchiôÙtecôÙturôÙal Style: Raw ConôÙcrete on the OutôÙside, High-End Parts on the Inside
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Meet the ãTelharmonium,ã the First Synthesizer (and Predecessor to Muzak), Invented in 1897
description: Before the New Year, we brought you footage of RussôÙian polyôÙmathôÙic invenôÙtor Lûˋon Theremin demonôÙstratôÙing the strange instruôÙment that bea
body:
Before the New Year, we brought you footage of RussôÙian polyôÙmathôÙic invenôÙtor Lûˋon Theremin demonôÙstratôÙing the strange instruôÙment that bears his surôÙname, and we notôÙed that the Theremin was the first elecôÙtronôÙic instruôÙment. This is not strictôÙly true, though it is the first elecôÙtronôÙic instruôÙment to be mass proôÙduced and wideôÙly used in origôÙiôÙnal comôÙpoôÙsiôÙtion and perôÙforôÙmance. But like bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal evoôÙluôÙtion, the hisôÙtoôÙry of musiôÙcal instruôÙment develôÙopôÙment is litôÙtered with dead ends, anomôÙalies, and forôÙgotôÙten ancesôÙtors (such as the octoôÙbass). One such obscure oddôÙiôÙty, the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum, appeared almost 20 years before the Theremin, and it was patentôÙed by its AmerôÙiôÙcan invenôÙtor, ThadôÙdeus Cahill, even earôÙliôÙer, in 1897. (See some of the many diaôÙgrams from the origôÙiôÙnal patent below.)

Cahill, a lawyer who had preôÙviôÙousôÙly inventôÙed devices for pianos and typeôÙwritôÙers, creôÙatôÙed the Telharmoniumãalso called the Dynamaphoneãto broadôÙcast music over the teleôÙphone, makôÙing it a preôÙcurôÙsor not to the Theremin but to the latôÙer scourge of teleôÙphone hold music. ãIn a large way,ã writes Jay WillisôÙton at Synthmuseum.com, ãCahill inventôÙed what we know of today as ãMuzak.ãã
He built the first proôÙtoôÙtype TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum, the Mark I, in 1901. It weighed sevôÙen tons. The final incarôÙnaôÙtion of the instruôÙment, the Mark III, took 50 peoôÙple to build at the cost of $200,000 and was ã60 feet long, weighed almost 200 tons and incorôÙpoôÙratôÙed over 2000 elecôÙtric switchôÙesãÎ. Music was usuôÙalôÙly played by two peoôÙple (4 hands) and conôÙsistôÙed of mostôÙly clasôÙsiôÙcal works by Bach, Chopin, Greig, RossiôÙni and othôÙers.ã The workôÙings of the garôÙganôÙtuôÙan machine resemôÙble the boilôÙer room of an indusôÙtriôÙal facilôÙiôÙty. (See sevôÙerôÙal phoôÙtographs here.)

NeedôÙless to say, this was a highôÙly impracôÙtiôÙcal instruôÙment. NevôÙerôÙtheôÙless, Cahill not only found willôÙing investors for the enorôÙmous conôÙtrapôÙtion, but he also staged sucôÙcessôÙful demonôÙstraôÙtions in BalôÙtiôÙmore, thenãafter disôÙasôÙsemôÙbling and movôÙing the thing by trainãin New York. By 1905, his New EngôÙland ElecôÙtric Music ComôÙpaôÙny ãmade a deal with the New York TeleôÙphone ComôÙpaôÙny to lay speôÙcial lines so that he could transôÙmit the sigôÙnals from the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum throughôÙout the city.ã Cahill used the term ãsynôÙtheôÙsizôÙingã in his patent, which some say makes the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum the first synôÙtheôÙsizôÙer, though its operôÙaôÙtion was as much mechanôÙiôÙcal as elecôÙtronôÙic, using a comôÙpliôÙcatôÙed series of gears and cylinôÙders to repliôÙcate the musiôÙcal range of a piano. (See the operôÙaôÙtion explained in the video at the top.) ãRaised bumps on cylinôÙders helped creôÙate musiôÙcal conôÙtour notes,ã writes PopôÙuôÙlar MechanôÙics, ãnot unlike a music box, with the size of the cylinôÙder deterôÙminôÙing the pitch.ã

The huge, very loud TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum Mark III endôÙed up in the baseôÙment of the MetôÙroôÙpolôÙiôÙtan Opera House for a time as Cahill worked on his scheme for pumpôÙing music through the teleôÙphone lines. But this planô did not come off smoothôÙly. ãThe probôÙlem was,ã PopôÙuôÙlar MechanôÙics points out,ã all cables leak off radio waves. SendôÙing a giganôÙtic, ampliôÙfied sigôÙnal on turn-of-the-20th-cenôÙtuôÙry phone lines was bound to cause trouôÙble.ã The TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum creôÙatôÙedô interôÙferôÙence on othôÙer phone lines and even interôÙruptôÙed Naval radio transôÙmisôÙsions. ãRumor has it,ã the DouôÙglas AnderôÙson School of the Arts writes, ãthat a New York busiôÙnessôÙman, infuôÙriôÙatôÙed by the conôÙstant netôÙwork interôÙferôÙence, broke into the buildôÙing where the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum was housed and destroyed it, throwôÙing pieces of the machinôÙery into the HudôÙson rivôÙer below.ã
The stoôÙry seems unlikeôÙly,ô but itô serves as a symôÙbol for the instruôÙmenôÙtãsô colôÙlapse. Cahillãs comôÙpaôÙny foldôÙed in 1908, though the final TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum supôÙposôÙedôÙly remained operôÙaôÙtional until 1916. No recordôÙings of the instruôÙment have surôÙvived, and ThadôÙdeus Cahillãs brothôÙer Arthur evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly sold the last proôÙtoôÙtype off for scrap in 1950 after failôÙing to find a buyôÙer. The entire ratioôÙnale for the instruôÙment had beenô supôÙplantôÙed by radio broadôÙcastôÙing. The TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum may have failed to catch on, but it still had a sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcant impact. Its unique design inspired anothôÙer imporôÙtant elecôÙtronôÙic instruôÙment, the HamôÙmond organ. And its very exisôÙtence gave musiôÙcal futurôÙists a vision. The DouôÙglas AnderôÙson School writes:
Despite its final demise, the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum trigôÙgered the birth of elecôÙtronôÙic musicãThe ItalôÙian ComôÙposôÙer and intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal FerôÙrucôÙcio Busoni inspired by the machine at the height of its popôÙuôÙlarôÙiôÙty was moved to write his ãSketch of a New AesôÙthetôÙic of Musicã (1907) which in turn became the clarôÙiôÙon call and inspiôÙraôÙtion for the new genôÙerôÙaôÙtion of elecôÙtronôÙic comôÙposers such as Edgard Varû´se and LuiôÙgi RusôÙsoôÙlo.
The instruôÙment also made quite an impresôÙsion on anothôÙer AmerôÙiôÙcan invenôÙtor, Mark Twain, who enthuôÙsiôÙasôÙtiôÙcalôÙlyô demonôÙstratôÙed it through the teleôÙphoneô durôÙingô a New Yearãs gathôÙerôÙing at his home, after givôÙing a speech about his own not inconôÙsidôÙerôÙable staôÙtus as an innoôÙvaôÙtor and earôÙly adopter of new techôÙnoloôÙgies. ãUnforôÙtuôÙnateôÙly for ThadôÙdeus Cahill,ã writes William Weir atô The HartôÙford Courant, ãTwainãs supôÙport wasôÙnãt enough to make a sucôÙcess of the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum.ã Learn more about the instruôÙmenôÙtãs hisôÙtoôÙry from thisô book.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
SoviôÙet InvenôÙtor Lûˋon Theremin Shows Off the Theremin, the EarôÙly ElecôÙtronôÙic InstruôÙment That Could Be Played WithôÙout Being Touched (1954)
The HisôÙtoôÙry of ElecôÙtronôÙic Music, 1800ã2015: Free Web Project CatôÙaôÙlogues the Theremin, Fairlight & OthôÙer InstruôÙments That RevôÙoôÙluôÙtionôÙized Music
The FasôÙciôÙnatôÙing StoôÙry of How the ElecôÙtric Music PioôÙneer Delia DerôÙbyshire CreôÙatôÙed the OrigôÙiôÙnalô DocôÙtor Whoô Theme (1963)
Hear SevôÙen Hours of Women MakôÙing ElecôÙtronôÙic Music (1938ã2014)
Thomas DolôÙby Explains How a SynôÙtheôÙsizôÙer Works on a Jim HenôÙson Kids Show (1989)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: A Lush Textile Installation Springs to Life in Shanghai
description: For the 13th edition of Design Shanghai, Hu Yuehua presented a bold bouquet intersecting organic forms and human craft traditions. ãWeaving Nature
body:
For the 13th edition of Design Shanghai, Hu Yuehua presented a bold bouquet intersecting organic forms and human craft traditions. ãWeaving Natureã is a large-scale composition of indigo and ochre leaves, blooms, and growths stitched together from dyed cotton and linen. Tightly nested in a wall-like garden, the individual pieces form a dense field of color and texture. Loose threads, raw edges, and tight rows of pleats radiate across the upright piece, merging evidence of the artistãs hand with the natural forms she depicts.
Design Shanghai concluded last week, but you can see more of the projects on display at the annual event on its website.

schema.headline: A Lush Textile Installation Springs to Life in Shanghai

title: Get 'Super/Natural' Inside Judith Schaechter's Stained Glass Sculpture
description: Like a miniature chapel with enough space for one person to stand comfortably, Judith Schaechterãs glowing installation, ãSuper/Natural,ã invites
body:
Like a miniature chapel with enough space for one person to stand comfortably, Judith Schaechterãs glowing installation, ãSuper/Natural,ã invites viewers to reflect on nature. An exhibition of the same name just opened at Claire Oliver Gallery and pays homage to biophilia, a theory positing that humans seek connections with nature through an innate attraction. Schaechter celebrates this propensity with a cornucopia of florals, insects, birds, and other imaginative organic forms.
ãThe vernacular of stained glass is one of worship and mythology,ã Schaechter says. ãSuper/Naturalô turns this a bit on its head, creating a secular sanctuary for contemplating beauty, nature, and our relationship to it.ã The sculpture, which comprises 65 panes and took nearly two years to complete, is topped with a small geodesic dome and stands about eight feet tall.

ãSuper/Naturalã came about partly as a result of Schaechterãs residency at the Penn Center for Neuroaesthetics, which focuses on a sub-discipline of cognitive neuroscience concerned with how the brain processes aesthetic experiences. The artist attended lab meetings with researchers and scientists and was influenced by explorations into the ãrelationships between art, beauty, morality, and the brain,ã the gallery says.
ãMy goal is to invite viewers into a deeply personal, immersive experience that explores the connections between self, nature, and imagination,ã Schaechter adds in a statement. ãWe are ultimately connected toãnot just observingãnature.ã
Super/Natural continues through May 23 in Harlem. Find more on the artistãs Instagram, along with insights into her research and process on her blog.

schema.headline: Get 'Super/Natural' Inside Judith Schaechter's Stained Glass Sculpture

title: Camille Lemoine's Portrait of Home and Belonging in Rural Scotland
description: Baldernock is a small parish located in the hills just north of Scotlandãs largest city. Itãs only seven miles between the village and Glasgow cit
body:
Baldernock is a small parish located in the hills just north of Scotlandãs largest city. Itãs only seven miles between the village and Glasgow city center, but its atmospheric moorland and rolling fields, dotted with sheep, feel a world away. For photographer Camille Lemoine, who currently lives in Glasgow and grew up in Bladernock, the familiar rhythms of small town life, agriculture, and the countryãs legendarily mercurial weather lend themselves to a series called Down Tower Road.
Intimate images capture steel gray clouds, gnarled trees, elegant grasses, and clusters of purple heather. Lemoine also emphasizes the presence of the female body, whether communing with the earth in a narrow track through a field or looking into the hollow of a tree as if physically merging with it.

This literal closeness to nature and the particularities of the landscape create what the artist describes as ãthe feeling of being moved into a different kind of aliveness.ã Lemoine explores both bodily and emotional connections to the land, highlighting curiosity and care in a study of what it means to really know a place.
ãThrough practicing the act of noticing, these images are devoted to the intricacies of the landscape, often bringing attention to the individuality of each thing, such as the blue before heavy rain, the pheasant feather found on the hill, and the distinct way that the light unfolds,ã Lemoine says in a statement. ãI want to highlight that it is our connection to these seemingly insignificant details that over time informs our sense of belonging.ã
Find more on Lemoineãs Instagram. (via LensCulture)

schema.headline: Camille Lemoine's Portrait of Home and Belonging in Rural Scotland
